what_you_see_or_feel: Concentric rings expanding from the bottom-left and bottom-right corners
what_in_the_music_drives_it: The deepest sub-bass frequencies — what you feel when an 808, a kick drum's body, or a sub-bass drop hits
how_we_measure_it: Energy in the 20-60 Hz range, extracted using a Mel spectrogram (a frequency-energy heat-map of the audio over time)
analysis_library: librosa.feature.melspectrogram — the standard frequency-band analysis used in music classification research and audio fingerprinting
why_this_choice: Sub-bass is felt as physical pressure, not heard as pitch. An expanding ring is the most direct possible visual metaphor for a pressure wave radiating outward — the same shape used to depict sonar pulses or shockwaves.

what_you_see_or_feel: Soft blurred mass drifting upward at the bottom of the screen
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Bass and low-mid energy — the rhythm-section foundation (bass guitar, kick drum, low synth pads)
how_we_measure_it: Energy in the 60-500 Hz range from the Mel spectrogram
why_this_choice: Bass is felt as mass and weight, not as a distinct shape. Edgeless, blurred, mass-without-silhouette imagery (smoke, fog) communicates "weight" without imposing a specific form.

what_you_see_or_feel: The smoke layers slosh side-to-side when the music gets noisy and stay still when it's musical
what_in_the_music_drives_it: How "musical" vs "noisy" the texture is at the moment
how_we_measure_it: Harmonic-percussive separation — the audio is mathematically split into its sustained-tonal part and its noise-percussive part, then the ratio is taken
analysis_library: librosa.effects.hpss — a standard music-analysis technique for separating melodic from percussive content
why_this_choice: Noisy moments feel chaotic; harmonic moments feel grounded. Lateral instability vs. stillness in the smoke directly mirrors that perceptual feel.

what_you_see_or_feel: A slow pulsing glow at the bottom of the screen — like the room is breathing
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Sub-bass presence + the song's overall energy/intensity level
how_we_measure_it: Sub-bass energy combined with a sine-wave breath whose rate scales with arousal (the music's energy estimate)
analysis_library: librosa.feature.melspectrogram + a music emotion model that estimates arousal
why_this_choice: Music never really stops between hits — there's always low-frequency presence. A breathing glow keeps the bottom of the screen alive even between events, and breath rate scaling with energy mimics how a listener's heart rate responds to intensity.

what_you_see_or_feel: Soft colored blobs spreading outward in the middle of the screen
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Sustained melodic content — the singing range, melody instruments, vocals
how_we_measure_it: Energy in the 250 Hz - 4 kHz range (the human voice + most melodic instruments live here), gated by how harmonic the music is
why_this_choice: Sustained notes don't "strike" — they bleed into each other. A blob that grows softly outward matches the temporal envelope of a held note far better than a sharp shape.

what_you_see_or_feel: The melody blobs drift left and right as the song's chord changes
what_in_the_music_drives_it: The currently dominant musical pitch class (C, C#, D, ...)
how_we_measure_it: A "chroma vector" extracts the strength of each of the 12 pitch classes from the audio every frame
analysis_library: librosa.feature.chroma_cqt — the standard pitch-class profile used in music key-detection algorithms (similar to Shazam-class song-matching)
why_this_choice: Tying horizontal position to the song's key means a chord change visibly pans the melodic body — chord changes become directly readable as motion.

what_you_see_or_feel: Blobs are round when the music is melodic and smear sideways when it gets noisy
what_in_the_music_drives_it: How harmonic (pitched) vs percussive/noisy the texture is
how_we_measure_it: Harmonic-percussive ratio
why_this_choice: Pitched sound is "organized" (well-defined frequency); noise is "chaotic" (energy spread across many frequencies). Round = organized; smeared = disorganized. Direct visual analog.

what_you_see_or_feel: Blobs sit higher when the song feels positive and lower when it feels sad
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Emotional valence — positive vs negative emotional tone
how_we_measure_it: A music emotion model trained on labeled musical examples scores the song's positive-vs-negative emotional character every half second
analysis_library: Backend emotion model (a small classifier trained on rated music; the same approach used in academic music-emotion research)
why_this_choice: We naturally associate "up" with positive ("things are looking up", "spirits lifted") and "down" with sadness. Vertical position is the most intuitive emotional encoding.

what_you_see_or_feel: Horizontal flowing ribbons of color across the top of the screen
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Air, brilliance, presence — the high-frequency "shimmer" content (cymbals, hi-hats, vocal sibilance, the sparkle of a guitar or string)
how_we_measure_it: Energy in the 4-10 kHz range from the Mel spectrogram
why_this_choice: High frequencies sit "above" in our perceptual map — they live in the head, not the gut. Flowing horizontal ribbons in the sky zone match both that spatial intuition and the "airy, flowing" character of the high-frequency band.

what_you_see_or_feel: More aurora ribbons appear when the music is rich in layered tones
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Combined energy and harmonic richness
how_we_measure_it: Backend arousal estimate plus harmonic-percussive ratio
why_this_choice: Rich harmonic content has many overlapping tones; visualizing as multiple stacked ribbons (each representing a tonal "voice") communicates that layering literally.

what_you_see_or_feel: Aurora ribbons cycle through colors faster when the song is energetic
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Arousal — how energetic the music feels
how_we_measure_it: Music emotion model scores arousal every half second
why_this_choice: Faster motion = more energy is universal across cultures (used by film scoring and advertising). Color-cycle rate provides a continuous intensity meter.

what_you_see_or_feel: Tiny sparkle specks high in the screen
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Ultra-high frequencies — cymbal sheen, hi-hat tip, vocal sibilance, shaker sparkle
how_we_measure_it: Energy in the 10-20 kHz range from the Mel spectrogram
why_this_choice: Ultra-highs feel crystalline, prickly, cold — the same character as ice crystals or frost. Tiny bright points capture that perceptual quality far better than any geometric shape.

what_you_see_or_feel: Specks float higher when the music is overall brighter
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Spectral centroid — the perceptual "brightness" of the sound (where energy sits in the frequency spectrum)
how_we_measure_it: The "center of mass" of the frequency spectrum, computed every frame
analysis_library: librosa.feature.spectral_centroid — a well-established psychoacoustic brightness measure
why_this_choice: Brighter sound is perceived as "higher" (we say "bright as a bell", "high notes"). Specks rising in the screen as brightness rises is a direct sensory analog.

what_you_see_or_feel: Bright particles erupt at a position determined by the hit's character — kick drums burst near the floor; hi-hats flash near the sky
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Drum hits and percussive attacks (called "onsets" in audio analysis)
how_we_measure_it: Onset detection algorithm finds moments when a new note/hit begins; the spectral brightness at that moment determines the vertical position
analysis_library: librosa.onset.onset_detect — the same onset detection used in DJ software and beat-grid editors
why_this_choice: Percussive attacks are kinetic and brief — particle bursts capture both. Bass-heavy hits mapping low and bright hits mapping high makes the kick-vs-cymbal distinction visible.

what_you_see_or_feel: Sharper hits produce denser particle bursts
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Attack sharpness — how abruptly a note starts
how_we_measure_it: The onset event includes a measurement of how steeply the volume rises at the attack
why_this_choice: A snare hit feels more startling than a soft mallet strike; the difference is visible as more vs fewer particles.

what_you_see_or_feel: On a percussive hit in a tonal passage the particles take the song's key color; in a noisy passage they take a neutral accent color
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Whether the hit is happening during a clearly-pitched moment (e.g. a melodic strike) or a noisy moment (e.g. a clap)
how_we_measure_it: Chroma strength — how strongly the music is in a recognizable key vs. just noise
why_this_choice: Pitched attacks "ignite" in the song's key; noisy attacks ignite in a neutral accent. Color encodes whether the hit is melodic or percussive.

what_you_see_or_feel: A soft radial bloom that lifts from the center on each beat
what_in_the_music_drives_it: The song's beat grid — when each tap of the rhythm lands
how_we_measure_it: Beat tracking estimates the underlying pulse of the music (the same algorithm class used in DJ software for beat-matching)
why_this_choice: A beat is emphasis, not a shape. A "feeling of brightness" in the center matches the felt punctuation of the rhythm without imposing a graphic primitive.

what_you_see_or_feel: On every fourth beat (the "1" of each measure), a wider ripple reaches the edges and picks up the song's key color
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Downbeats — the strongest beat in each measure
how_we_measure_it: Downbeat detection identifies which beats are the "1" vs the "2, 3, 4"
why_this_choice: Downbeats carry more weight in music theory (they're "the one"); a wider ripple matches the felt weight, and tinting it with the key personalizes the emphasis.

what_you_see_or_feel: Radial streaks point away from a moving anchor — when the melody rises the anchor moves up-right; when it falls down-left
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Pitch direction — whether the melody is going up or down
how_we_measure_it: We track how the spectral brightness changes over time; a rising trend means rising pitch
why_this_choice: We say "the melody is going up" — pitch direction is already a spatial metaphor in language. Rays pointing where the melody is going makes the metaphor literal.

what_you_see_or_feel: Short angular slashes flash across the middle horizontal band on sudden spectral changes
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Spectral flux spikes — moments when the sound suddenly changes character (snare cracks, sample stabs, stuttery synth chops)
how_we_measure_it: We compute how much the frequency content changes from frame to frame, then flag moments that exceed an adaptive threshold (mean + 1.8 standard deviations)
analysis_library: librosa.onset.onset_strength (the same flux measure that powers onset detection)
why_this_choice: A sudden spectral change is a "rupture" — slashes read as "something broke" while round sparkles read as continuous shimmer. Distinct visual vocabulary keeps these events readable as a third event type, separate from beats and onsets.

what_you_see_or_feel: A subtle warping of the entire scene, like heat haze rising off pavement
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Chaotic, high-energy moments — drops, dense climaxes, distortion-heavy passages
how_we_measure_it: Spectral flux (rate of change) plus the drop state machine
why_this_choice: Intense pressure literally distorts air (heat haze, shockwaves); applying the same effect to the scene preserves that physical analog.

what_you_see_or_feel: Subtle TV-static noise across the whole screen, intensifying during chaotic passages, with a cool blue tint when the music feels anxious or sad
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Spectral disorder + emotional valence
how_we_measure_it: Spectral flux plus emotion model valence
why_this_choice: Disorder = noise; that's a direct audio-to-visual translation. Adding a cool tint during low-valence passages encodes anxiety/sadness without requiring a separate texture.

what_you_see_or_feel: An iridescent oil-slick wash around the edges of the screen with up to 12 hue-stops corresponding to the 12 musical pitch classes
what_in_the_music_drives_it: The chord/key currently active. A C major chord paints C/E/G prominently; a tritone paints two opposite arcs.
how_we_measure_it: The chroma vector — the strength of each of the 12 pitch classes — extracted from the audio every frame; each pitch class gets its own hue stop weighted by its chroma magnitude
analysis_library: librosa.feature.chroma_cqt — the standard pitch-class profile used in music key-detection algorithms
why_this_choice: Musical key is colored light, not form — it should drive hue, not shape. An iridescent wash that appears when tonal and disappears when atonal lets the listener literally see harmony fade in and out.

what_you_see_or_feel: A vertical bar on the left side of the screen with 12 color stops; the active stop glows
what_in_the_music_drives_it: The dominant pitch class of the moment ("the song is in F# right now")
how_we_measure_it: The chroma vector's strongest pitch class
why_this_choice: A persistent dashboard indicator gives the deaf user a learnable, glanceable readout of the key — more direct than a tinted scene. 12 stops match the 12 pitch classes of Western music.

what_you_see_or_feel: 8 horizontal rungs stacked vertically on the left side; the active rung climbs as the music brightens
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Spectral centroid — perceptual brightness (where in the spectrum the energy lives)
how_we_measure_it: librosa.feature.spectral_centroid, normalized per-track to the 5th-95th percentile range
why_this_choice: A discrete brightness meter is the cleanest possible reading of "where in the spectrum the energy lives" — like a VU meter for brightness. 8 rungs match the 8 frequency bands.

what_you_see_or_feel: A soft hexagonal mesh on the right side that crystallizes when the music is harmonic and dissolves when noisy
what_in_the_music_drives_it: How organized vs chaotic the spectrum is
how_we_measure_it: Harmonic-percussive ratio
why_this_choice: Harmonic sound is structurally organized (overtones aligning to integer ratios); noise is structurally fragmented. A mesh that holds together vs falls apart is the most direct visual analog of "structural integrity".

what_you_see_or_feel: Soft warm glows in the top-left + bottom-right corners (warm diagonal) when the song feels positive; soft cool glows in the top-right + bottom-left corners (cool diagonal) when the song feels sad
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Emotional valence (positive vs negative emotional tone)
how_we_measure_it: Music emotion model scores the song's emotional positivity
analysis_library: Backend emotion model trained on labeled musical examples
why_this_choice: Valence is a slow scalar — it should appear as a persistent compositional weight, not a motion event. Warm-vs-cool color is a deeply learned cultural association with happy-vs-sad.

what_you_see_or_feel: The corner glows open wider when the song is energetic and contract when it's calm
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Arousal — the song's energy/intensity level
how_we_measure_it: Music emotion model scores arousal
why_this_choice: Bloom radius scaling with energy lets the corners breathe with the song's intensity without crowding the scene.

what_you_see_or_feel: The background of the entire scene gets brighter when the music is loud overall
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Overall loudness
how_we_measure_it: Root mean square of the audio waveform — the same loudness measure used by every audio level meter
why_this_choice: Brightness = loudness is one of the oldest cross-modal mappings (a "loud" color, a "bright" sound). Lifts the whole scene with the song's volume without requiring any specific texture to react.

what_you_see_or_feel: The scene composes differently depending on whether you're in the intro, verse, chorus, bridge, breakdown, drop, or outro — chorus mirrors horizontally; bridge rotates 12 degrees and shifts hue; breakdown desaturates and goes near-monochrome; drop super-saturates
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Song structure — the named sections of a pop song
how_we_measure_it: A music structure-segmentation algorithm identifies repeated sections and labels them
analysis_library: Backend section-segmentation (typically MSAF or a similar standard library)
why_this_choice: Each section of a song has a different emotional function (intro builds, chorus declares, bridge wanders, drop releases, outro fades). The scene's composition shifts accordingly so the listener can read where they are in the song's arc.

what_you_see_or_feel: At the moment of a bass drop, the scene's color palette briefly inverts and a particle escape burst flies past the edges
what_in_the_music_drives_it: The peak "release" moment of an EDM-style drop or a major climax
how_we_measure_it: Drop state machine triggers on (a) entering a section labeled "drop" or (b) heuristic conditions firing simultaneously: high arousal AND high spectral flux AND high energy
analysis_library: Backend section labels + custom drop detector
why_this_choice: Drops are scored moments — the song's biggest payoff. A pre-authored choreography (palette flip + escape burst + heat distortion) gives the moment the felt drama it deserves.

what_you_see_or_feel: A continuous low rumble in the device that varies in intensity with the bass
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Sub-bass + bass energy
how_we_measure_it: Sum of energy in the 20-250 Hz range
why_this_choice: Bass is felt physically in the body — a continuous haptic rumble reproduces that physical bass sensation directly. Capped intensity prevents the hum from masking sharper rhythmic events.

what_you_see_or_feel: A strong, sharp tap on the strongest beat of each measure (the "1")
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Downbeats — the structurally strongest beats
how_we_measure_it: Beat tracking + downbeat detection
why_this_choice: Downbeats anchor the listener's sense of rhythm; a stronger tap lets the deaf user feel the song's pulse and predict the next downbeat.

what_you_see_or_feel: A lighter tap on the other beats in each measure (the "2, 3, 4")
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Off-beats — the secondary beats in the measure
how_we_measure_it: Same beat tracker
why_this_choice: Lighter taps preserve the felt difference between the strong "1" and the lighter "2, 3, 4" — the same dynamic that human drummers play.

what_you_see_or_feel: A subtle secondary tap on percussive hits between beats — snare, claps, off-grid drum hits
what_in_the_music_drives_it: Note onsets that aren't on the main beat grid
how_we_measure_it: Onset detection + attack-slope features
why_this_choice: A real drum kit isn't just kick-snare on beats; there's syncopation, rolls, fills. Onset taps add this granularity so the deaf user feels the full rhythmic detail, not just the metronome.

what_you_see_or_feel: A pre-authored haptic envelope (multi-event sequence) fires at the moment of a bass drop
what_in_the_music_drives_it: The peak release moment of a drop or major climax
how_we_measure_it: Drop state machine (same trigger as the visual drop choreography)
analysis_library: Backend section labels + custom drop detector
why_this_choice: Drops are scored moments. A pre-authored, richer envelope (instead of a single tap) gives the haptic the same dramatic weight the visual gets.
